segments.s214-g41: کنون (now) is a temporal adverb placed at the start to set the scene. کامدی is an archaic/poetic present/presentive verb form of کامد (“comes”); here it indicates that the men's deeds are coming to pass. کار مردان is the noun phrase “the work/deeds of men,” and ببین is an imperative “see/observe,” commanding the addressee to notice the situation; the verb-final/object-verb order typical of Persian is inverted poetically (verb preceding subject/object elements).
segments.s214-g42: نه گاه فریبست is a short nominal clause: نه + گاه (‘not a time’) + فریبست (poetic copular form meaning ‘it is deception’ or ‘it is a deceitful time’), so literally ‘it is not a deceptive/time.’ The connector و links the next nominal phrase روز کمین (‘a day of ambush’), where روز is the head noun and کمین is its genitive attribute; the copula is understood for the second phrase (ellipsis of ‘is’).
segments.s214-g43: چو (when) governs the temporal clause; بشنید is a past tense verb form ‘heard’ (in epic Persian a singular-form verb can accompany a plural subject), followed by the subject پیران (‘the elders’). ز هر سو سپاه is an adverbial/object phrase ‘an army/from every side,’ with ز هر سو marking source or direction.
segments.s214-g44: فرستاد is a simple past ‘sent/dispatch’d’ (here with purposeful force), و is the coordinating conjunction, and بگرفت is past ‘seized/took hold (of).’ بر + کوه + راه gives the place/route: بر کوه راه = ‘the road on/to the mountain’ (بر used here as directional/locational), so the clause says they sent troops and took possession of the mountain road.
segments.s214-g45: بهر سو (toward that side) is a directional phrase placed before the source phrase ز توران (‘from Turan’). بیامد is the past simple ‘came,’ and گروه follows as the subject (‘a group/troop’), showing a verb–subject ordering typical in poetic narrative.
segments.s214-g46: سپاه انجمن کرد is a compact clause: سپاه (the army/host) + انجمن (an assembly/meeting) + کرد (did/made) — literally ‘the army made an assembly,’ i.e., the host gathered. بر گرد کوه is a locative phrase ‘around the mountain’ with بر گرد = around/about and کوه the noun modified.
segments.s214-g47: بریشان is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning ‘for them’ or ‘to them’ (built on بر + ایشان); چو راه علف تنگ شد is a temporal clause ‘when the path of grass grew narrow,’ with شد as the past copula ‘became.’ Note the adjective تنگ follows the noun phrase راه علف and the whole predicate follows چو.
segments.s214-g48: سپهبد (the spahbed, a general) is the subject; سوی is a directional preposition ‘toward,’ and چاره جنگ is a genitival phrase ‘a remedy/means of fight,’ with شد (‘became’/‘turned to’) functioning to indicate that the spahbed turned his attention toward finding a way to fight — a literary succinct expression for ‘he set about planning the battle.’
segments.s214-g1: فرستاده (subject) + نزدیک پیران is a locative phrase modifying where he arrived; رسید is a simple past verb 'arrived/reached.' In older Persian the locative phrase can follow the noun without a preposition (here 'نزدیک پیران' = 'near Piran').
segments.s214-g2: بجوشید is a past (narrative) verb meaning 'seethed/boiled' used here figuratively for strong emotion; چون is a conjunction 'as/when' introducing the temporal clause; گفت functions as the reported-speech verb 'said' (the dictionary senses offered are conversational but here it is the past of گفتن); هومان is the proper name; شنید is past 'heard.' The clause reads 'he seethed when he heard Homan speak.'
segments.s214-g3: بیامد is past 'came'; شب تیره is a noun + adjective ('night, dark') with the adjective following the noun in normal Persian order; هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase 'at the time/hour of sleep' specifying when the night came.
segments.s214-g4: همی is a narrative/emphatic particle often marking continuous or habitual action in classical Persian and precedes the verb; راند is the past form 'drove' (transitive); لشکر is the object 'the army'; بکردار is an adjectival/adverbial phrase 'well‑ordered / of good conduct' modifying how the army was driven; آب indicates the destination 'toward the water' (the preposition 'به' is omitted but implied). Word order: [particle] + verb + object + manner + destination.
segments.s214-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; زان is an archaic demonstrative/adverb 'from that' (zān); چادر قیرگون is a noun + adjective ('veil/cloak, pitch‑black') where the adjective follows the noun. The phrase means 'when the sun emerged from that pitch‑black veil.'
segments.s214-g6: غمی شد uses شد as a light verb 'became' with غمی 'a grief' as subject (i.e., 'grief arose'); بدرید is a transitive/past verbal form 'tore' or 'was torn' and here describes grief bursting forth; و آمد برون is the compound 'and came out/forth' with آمد (came) + adverb برون (out). The sequence describes grief breaking loose and emerging.
segments.s214-g7: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a military commander); بکوه is a locative phrase 'to the mountain' (بـ = 'to'); هماون is a demonstrative 'that/same' emphasizing which mountain; رسید is the past 'reached/arrived.' Word order places the locative before the demonstrative and verb.
segments.s214-g8: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (or 'because of'); گرد سپه here means 'the dust/cloud stirred up by the army' (گرد = dust/swirl, سپه = host/army); کوه شد ناپدید is an intransitive result clause 'the mountain became unseen/vanished' with شد as 'became' and ناپدید 'unseen/vanished.' Together: 'from the army's dust the mountain was made unseen.'
segments.s214-g25: ‘گریزان’ is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'fleeing' and stands before the coordinated noun phrase 'و لشکر' (and the army). 'پس' is a temporal/adverbial 'then' that anticipates what follows; 'اندر دمان' is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/within the skirt/foothill'). Word order is typical classical Persian: modifiers and short adverbials often precede the locative phrase.
segments.s214-g26: ‘بدام’ here is an archaic/poetic spelling of Persian ‘به دام’ ('into the snare'); it precedes the verb phrase. 'اندر' marks location 'in', 'آیی' is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن ('you come' / 'you will come'), and 'همی' is the classical verbal particle that adds an ongoing/assertive aspect to the verb. 'بی‌گمان' is a sentence-final adverb 'undoubtedly/without a doubt'.
segments.s214-g27: ‘چنین’ is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' setting the form of the reply. 'داد' is the simple past 'gave', and together 'داد پاسخ' is the fixed verbal phrase 'gave an answer/replied'. 'سرافراز' is an epithet 'proud/triumphant' placed before the name 'طوس' (Tūs).
segments.s214-g28: The conjunction 'که' introduces reported content ('that'). 'من' is the first-person subject 'I'. 'بر' is a preposition here meaning roughly 'against/about' in the phrase 'بر دروغ تو' ('about/against your lie'). 'دارم' is the present-tense verb 'I have'; 'فسوس' is the noun meaning a secret misgiving/insidious whisper, so the idiom 'دارم فسوس' means 'I have a misgiving'.
segments.s214-g29: ‘پی’ here carries the notion of following/pursuit ('in pursuit of' / 'following'), linked to the noun 'کین' ('hatred, vengeance'). 'تو' is the second-person subject 'you' and 'افگندی' is the past tense second-person singular 'you cast/you threw'. 'اندر جهان' is a locative phrase 'into/throughout the world'. The verb is transitive and the sequence is subject+verb+locative.
segments.s214-g30: ‘ز بهر’ is a classical causal/benefactive phrase 'for the sake of / because of'. It governs the personal name 'سیاوش' (Siavash). 'میان مهان' is another locative phrase 'among the guests' (میان = among, مهان = guests). Together they specify motive and the social setting.
segments.s214-g31: ‘برین گونه’ is the fused prepositional phrase 'بر این گونه' meaning 'in this manner' (بر = on/upon, ین = demonstrative 'this', گونه = 'manner/way'). 'تا چند' is an idiomatic classical question 'how long' ('تا' here contributes the 'until/how long' sense). 'گویی' in context functions as 'you speak / you say' (classical present form), and 'دروغ' is the object 'lie'.
segments.s214-g32: ‘دروغت’ is 'your lie' with the possessive suffix -ت. 'بر ما نگیرد' uses 'بر' + pronoun 'ما' and the negative present/subjunctive 'نگیرد' (literally 'may it not seize/take'); here it is optative/imperative in tone: 'may it not take hold on us.' 'فروغ' ('radiance, splendor') is the thing threatened by the lie, placed sentence-finally as the affected noun.
segments.s214-g81: ز introduces the source ('from Goudarz'). The clause uses a simple past verb بشنید (3rd singular) 'he heard'; word order places the source phrase ز گودرز before the verb, then the subject طوس and the object این سخن follow, yielding 'From Goudarz, Tūs heard this speech.'
segments.s214-g82: سرش is 'his head' with ش as the possessive clitic. گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became' here. پردرد is an adjectival descriptor 'sorrowful/painful', joined by و to the noun phrase کین کهن ('old grudge/hatred'), so the whole means 'his head became sorrowful and (full of) an ancient grudge.'
segments.s214-g83: ز یک سوی لشکر is an ablative/partitive phrase 'from / of one side of the army' (ز marking the part). The verb سپرد (past 3rd sg.) 'entrusted/handed over' takes the indirect object به بیژن 'to Bijan', so 'he entrusted one side of the army to Bijan.'
segments.s214-g84: دگر سو 'the other side' is a parallel phrase; the remainder names the persons to whom it is given. In classical poetic prose the preposition to/be- may be fused or elided, so بشیدوش و خراد are the persons for the other side and گرد here functions as a verbal/poetic ending implying assignment/turning over (an archaic/poetic use). The clause thus completes the contrast: one side to Bijan, the other to Shidush and Kharad.
segments.s214-g85: درفش خجسته is the noun 'the auspicious banner/standard' with the adjective after it. بگستهم is the infinitival/imperative stem meaning 'to spread/unfurl', and داد functions causatively/predicatively here ('gave' / 'caused'), so the compound means roughly 'he had the auspicious banner unfurled' or 'he spread the auspicious banner.'
segments.s214-g86: بسی functions as an intensifier 'much/many' in this context (classical use). پند و اندرزها is the coordinated object 'advice and counsels' (اندرزها is the plural), and کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد 'did/made' with یاد 'remembrance/mention' yields 'recalled/uttered' — thus 'he recalled many pieces of advice and counsel.'
segments.s214-g73: بباید is an impersonal modal ('it is necessary' / 'must'); here it governs the following infinitive گزیدن ('to choose'). سواران مرد is the object: سواران 'riders/horsemen' followed by مرد used attributively after the noun to mean 'valiant/heroic men' (adjective-like), so the phrase = 'choose valiant horsemen.'
segments.s214-g74: This phrase gives the direction/purpose of the choosing. ز + verbal noun بالا شدن ('the act of going up / being on high') marks origin or position ('from the high ground'); سوی دشت is a prepositional phrase 'toward the plain'; نبرد here is the battlefield/engagement noun ('battle'). Grammatically the whole unit expresses 'from the high place toward the plain for battle'.
segments.s214-g75: بسان 'like/as' compares what follows to a night-attack (شبیخون). یکی رزم سخت is a noun phrase 'a single/focussed fierce battle' — note Persian adjective order: رزم (battle) + سخت (severe/fierce). The whole phrase = 'like a night-raid, a single fierce battle.'
segments.s214-g76: بسازیم is 1st-person-plural hortatory/subjunctive ('let us make/prepare'); تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that'); چون بود here means 'when/if it is the case that' and is followed by یار بخت ('fortune as ally') — literally 'when fortune is a friend/ally.' Note the tense/aspect is generic/potential rather than a simple past.
segments.s214-g77: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. یک بیک تن is an emphatic way to say 'one chieftain/person' (بیک 'bey, chieftain' + تن 'person' as a single unit). بکشتن دهیم combines the infinitive بکشتن ('to kill') with دهیم ('we give/causatively effect') — here meaning 'we succeed in causing to be killed' or 'we kill / cause to be killed.'
segments.s214-g78: وگر ('but if' / 'or if') presents the alternative. تاج گردنکشان is a genitive/grouping 'the crowns of the proud (or rebellious ones)'. برنهیم is a verb form expressing the action on that object (here contextually 'we strip / put low / remove' their crowns); grammatically it is the clause's verb in the same conditional frame.
segments.s214-g79: چنین است is a fixed copular phrase 'thus it is' / 'such is'. فرجام آوردگاه is a genitive phrase 'the outcome/end (فرجام) of the battlefield (آوردگاه)' — noun + noun in genitive relation, so the line states a general verdict about battle.
segments.s214-g80: یکی ... یکی ... gives a contrasting pair: 'one (person) finds ... another (finds) ...'. خاک یابد literally 'finds dust/earth' (i.e. is buried/defeated); یکی تاج 'another the crown'. و گاه at the end ('and گاه') functions adverbially as 'and sometimes' or 'and at times', noting variability of outcomes.
segments.s214-g49: Literal order is poetic: the verb گفت appears before the subject هومان. چنین is a sentence adverb meaning 'thus/this way'. بپیران is composed of the dative prefix بـ (be-, 'to') + پیران ('elders'), marking the indirect object 'to the elders'. گرد here is an archaic/directional particle attached after the dative phrase (functionally 'to' / 'toward'), so the segment reads 'Homan said thus to the elders.'
segments.s214-g50: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). ما را uses را as the object marker ('us' = direct object). پی before a place noun has the sense 'after / toward / following' (here 'toward' the mountain). کوه is the destination. باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/ought', and سپرد (infinitive/past stem) is used intransitively/passively to mean 'to be entrusted/sent' (i.e. 'must be sent/entrusted to the mountain').
segments.s214-g51: یکی here means 'one' / 'a single' (i.e. 'one battle'). جنگ is the noun 'battle/war.' سازیم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/jussive of ساختن used to propose or resolve an action: 'let us make / we will make.' کایرانیان is vocative/plural 'O Kayanids' or the group addressed.
segments.s214-g52: نبندند is a negative 3rd-person-plural jussive/subjunctive ('they should not bind/they will not close'). ازین پس is the temporal phrase 'from now on' (از + این written archaically as ین + پس 'after/from then'). بکینه is بـ + کینه meaning 'with hatred / in enmity', and میان means 'between.' Together: 'so that (they) will not, from now on, bind/close the hatred between (them),' i.e. will not end the feud.
segments.s214-g53: بدو is an enclitic/old dative 'to him' (be + او contracted), introducing the indirect object. گفت is the verb 'said' (verb form not listed among provided senses). پیران is the plural noun 'elders.' که introduces reported content. بر ماست is a copular construction: بر = 'upon', ما = 'us', ست = archaic copula 'is' (third-person), and باد is the optative particle 'may'/'let it be so' used here to express obligation or wish — together 'it should be upon us' / 'it ought to be ours.'
segments.s214-g54: نکردست is an older/perfect negative form meaning 'has not done' or 'there is not' (here best read 'no one has'). با = 'with / by'. باد in this clause is literal 'wind' (not the optative particle), so با باد = 'by/with the wind'. کس = 'anyone / anyone at all'. رزم = 'war / warfare'. یاد here is a noun 'remembrance/knowledge' (poetically used to mean 'learning' or 'skill'). The clause says essentially 'no one has (learned) warfare by the wind.'
segments.s214-g55: ز is the poetic/archaic form of از meaning 'from.' جنگ = 'battle.' پیاده here means 'on foot' or 'the infantry' (nominative/agent). بپیچید is a past-tense 3rd-person-singular of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ, meaning 'turned aside / veered off.' سر here is 'head' or 'chief' and in this condensed, poetic line functions as the subject/point of action or as 'their head(s)' — overall: 'from the battle the infantry/footmen turned away.'
segments.s214-g56: شود is the 3rd-person-singular present/subjunctive of شدن meaning 'becomes / will be.' تیره is an adjective 'dark / ominous.' دیدار is 'meeting / encounter.' پرخاشخر is a poetically formed adjective/noun meaning 'hostile / quarrelsome' (a hostile encounter). Together: 'the meeting became dark and hostile.'
segments.s214-g17: خروشید is an archaic/poetic past tense verb meaning 'roared/ shouted' (3rd person); کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; نامبردار is an adjectival epithet 'the named/renowned' modifying the following proper name; طوس is the name being addressed. Word order is Verb + Vocative phrase (poetic inversion) so the verb announces the shout and the following phrase states its content.
segments.s214-g18: This is a nominal apposition describing طوس: خداوند here means 'lord/sovereign' (an honorific applied to a person), governing a coordinated list of nouns (پیلان و گوپال و کوس). The conjunction و simply links the items; the whole phrase functions as an epithet in apposition to Tus.
segments.s214-g19: کنون is an adverb 'now/presently'. ماهیان is the plural noun 'fishes' (here poetic imagery). اندر آمد is a compound of the preposition اندر 'into/in' and the past verb آمد 'came', giving 'came in/entered'. به + پنج literally 'to/into five' here means 'in fives' or 'in groups of five' (poetic numeric expression follows the preposition).
segments.s214-g20: که تا is a purposive/relative particle cluster meaning 'so that' or 'in order that'. تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun. همی is the old verbal prefix/particle marking ongoing or habitual action (presentive). رزم (object) 'battle' followed by جویی, older present/stative form of 'seek' (here 'you seek'). برنج is a bare noun placed after the verb phrase as the object/goal — literally 'rice' (poetic; the relation is 'seeking battle for rice'). Overall this is a purpose clause: 'so that you continually seek battle for rice.'
segments.s214-g21: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from/among'. گودرزیان is the plural proper noun 'the Gudarzis'. آن is the demonstrative 'those/the'. کجا is interrogative/exclamatory 'where'. مهترند is a present indicative/copula construction (مهتر + اند) meaning 'they are chiefs/seniors' or 'they are greater'. The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'From the Gudarzis, those where are they great?' → 'Of the Gudarzis, where are those who are leaders?'
segments.s214-g22: بدان is a fused locative/form 'به + آن' meaning 'to/at that' (here rendered as 'at that'). رزمگاهت is the possessed noun 'your battlefield' (رزمگاه + enclitic -ت). همه 'all' is the subject/topic. بی‌سرند is a predicative adjective with the negative prefix بی- 'without' plus سر 'head' and the 3rd‑person plural copula -ند, meaning 'they are headless' (i.e., slain or without leaders).
segments.s214-g23: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun. چون is the comparative/simile 'like/as'. غرم is a poetic noun 'wrath/anger' used as the term of comparison. رفتستی is the archaic past 2nd‑person singular form 'you went'. اندر کمر is a prepositional phrase 'into the waist/center/line' (کمر here denotes the midline or the ranks). The clause describes manner ('you went like wrath into the centre').
segments.s214-g24: This is a paired adjectival description using repeated "پر از" ('full of') phrases. پر از داوری دل = 'a heart full of judgment' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', داوری = 'judgment', دل = 'heart'). Then پر از کینه سر = 'a head full of hatred' (سر = 'head'). The nominal phrases are juxtaposed without an explicit copula—typical poetic ellipsis—so they describe the subject (you) as possessing those qualities.
segments.s214-g65: چنین (thus) + گفت (said) opens the clause; the verb 'گفت' comes before its subject in this poetic word order. با طوس ('to/with Tous') marks the addressee (با here = 'to' in speech), and 'گودرز پیر' is the speaker's name with the epithet 'پیر' ('the old/elder') placed after it as an appositive subject.
segments.s214-g66: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). ما را uses را to mark the experiencer/affected party ('for us'); کنون is a temporal adverb ('now'). جنگ is the subject of شد ('became') and شد is the past form of شدن meaning 'became'; ناگزیر is a predicate adjective 'inevitable'. Together: 'that for us now the war became inevitable.'
segments.s214-g67: سه روز ('three days') gives the quantity; ار is an archaic conditional equivalent to اگر ('if'). بود is the past/conditional form 'were/was'; خوردنی is a noun formed from خوردن here meaning 'provisions/food'; بیش نیست means 'there is no more' (بیش as 'more' + نیست 'does not exist'). The whole clause = 'if there were food for three days, there is no more than that.'
segments.s214-g68: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'on'; یکسو 'one side'. گشاده modifies رهی (an adjectival order: 'an open way/path'). پیش here means 'ahead' or 'in front', and نیست negates existence: 'there is no open road ahead on one side.' Note the adjective precedes the noun (گشاده رهی) in this older style.
segments.s214-g69: The paired negatives نه ... و نه ... produce a series 'neither ... nor ...': خورد (here functioning as 'provisions'), چیز ('things/anything'), بار ('loads/supplies') and بنه ('bundles'). The items are listed with repeated نه for emphasis: 'neither food nor anything nor baggage and bundles.'
segments.s214-g70: چنین چند = 'so few'/'only this many' (a quantifying phrase). باشد is a classical optative/subjunctive form often rendered as 'will be' or 'will remain' in consequence clauses; here it indicates the resulting state. سپه 'the host/army' is the subject and گرسنه 'hungry' is the predicate adjective: 'the army will (remain) hungry if there are so few (supplies).'
segments.s214-g71: کنون ('now') sets the time. چون شود here functions temporally as 'when/as it becomes': چون + شود introduces a temporal clause. روی خورشید ('the sun's face') is the subject and زرد describes its color/state ('turns yellow/pale'). The clause reads 'now, when the sun's face becomes yellow...'
segments.s214-g72: پدید آید is a verb phrase meaning 'appears/comes into view' (پدید + آید). آن چادر لاژورد is a noun phrase 'that tent [of] lapis/azure' (لاژورد indicating the lapis/azure color). Word order is VSO here (verb before its noun phrase), a common poetic inversion: 'appears that lapis-colored tent.'
segments.s214-g57: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. راه علف تنگ شد is a verbal clause with the adjective تنگ after the noun phrase راه علف ("grassy way") and شد (become) as the past/resultative auxiliary: 'the grassy way became narrow.' بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase ('for/upon the host') indicating who is affected; in older Persian placement after the verb is common in poetry.
segments.s214-g58: کسی ... ندارد نگاه is negative existential phrasing: کسی combined with the present-tense ندارد ('does not have') yields 'no one had/possessed (a) look' — i.e. 'no one looked.' کوه خارا is a noun phrase 'the rocky/flinty mountain' (خارا modifying کوه). The object/phrase نگاه ('a look, sight, gaze') follows ندارد, a common nominal object after داشتن; the sense is idiomatic: 'no one cast a gaze toward the rocky mountain.'
segments.s214-g59: همه لشکر آید is a conditional/optative idea 'if the whole host comes' with آید in the simple-present/optative sense. بزنهار ما is an archaic fused expression meaning something like 'to our rescue' or 'for our warning/help' (بزنهار is an old literary word); ما is the beneficiary 'us.' The whole segment expresses the protasis 'if the whole army should come to our aid.'
segments.s214-g60: ازین پس is the adverbial 'from now on' (از + این + پس; here ین is the orthographic variant of این). نجویند پیکار ما is a negative-future/habitual claim with نجویند (3rd plural present/subjunctive in classical style) 'they will not seek/pursue' and پیکار ما 'battle against us' as its object; word order places the object after the verb, typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s214-g61: بریشان is a contracted poetic form of بر ایشان ('toward them' / 'for them'), so بریشان کنون جای بخشایش است literally 'for them now is the place/time of forgiveness.' کنون جای بخشایش است is a copular clause with است 'is' and جای بخشایش 'a place/time for mercy/forgiveness.'
segments.s214-g62: نه هنگام پیکار و آرایش است is a negative parallel clause: نه ... است ('it is not ...') followed by هنگام پیکار و آرایش 'the time for battle and array/mustering.' آرایش here means 'array/arrangement' (military array), so the clause denies that this is a moment for fighting or for arranging troops.
segments.s214-g63: رسید این سگالش بگودرز و طوس is a straightforward verbal clause: رسید 'reached/arrived' with این سگالش 'this message/signal' as its theme and بگودرز و طوس marking the persons affected ('to Goudarz and Tous' — in poetry the dative/recipient is expressed with the prefix ب-).
segments.s214-g64: سر سرکشان خیره گشت از فسوس is idiomatic: سر ("head/chief") + سرکشان (here 'the leaders' or 'the headstrong') forms the subject, خیره گشت is the copular/resultative 'became astonished/stood staring,' and از فسوس 'because of/from the whisperings' gives the cause. The whole means the chiefs were struck aghast by the murmured talk; note the poetic inversion and compressed morphology common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s214-g9: بهومان is the speaker's name (subject) in the nominative; چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so' modifying گفت). کز is an archaic/poetic conjunction (contracted form) introducing the content of the speech ('that' / 'that from ...'); رزمگاه is the place noun 'battlefield' that sets the scene for the following clause.
segments.s214-g10: مجنب و مجنبان are imperatives formed from جنب (to move) used emphatically here (singular and plural forms, addressing individuals and the host) meaning 'do not move / do not stir'. از + ایدر is a prepositional phrase 'from here' (از = 'from', ایدر = 'here'). سپاه names the addressee/topic 'the army' and is placed after the prepositional phrase in this poetic ordering.
segments.s214-g11: شوم is the 1st person subjunctive/optative of شدن meaning 'may I become / let me (go/come/ be able)' and here introduces the speaker's wish or intention. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. سپهدار ایرانیان is a genitive noun phrase 'the commander (سپهدار) of the Iranians' functioning as the logical patient/topic of the following clause.
segments.s214-g12: چه دارد is 'what (چه) has (دارد)'; the verb دارد is present-tense 'has' and governs the question word چه. بپا here is a verbal/adjectival element meaning 'to bring about / to be raised / to happen' (poetic use), and اختر کاویان 'the Kayanian star' is the agent/theme whose action is being asked about. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'چه دارد بپا اختر کاویان' = 'what has the Kayanian star to bring about'.
segments.s214-g13: بکوه is a prepositional phrase 'to the mountain' (به + کوه) and here points to a particular place; هماون is a demonstrative/adjectival 'that same' (referring back to the mountain just mentioned). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that', and دادش is the past-tense verb داد 'gave' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' ('gave him'). نوید is the noun 'tidings / good news' as the direct object of داد.
segments.s214-g14: بدین بودن is a poetic phrase meaning 'being thus / to be in this state' (here expressed as a gerund/nominal clause); اکنون is the adverb 'now'. چه دارد امید is a rhetorical question 'what hope does (he) have': چه = 'what', دارد = present 'has', امید = 'hope'. Note the omission of an explicit subject (he) and the poetic ordering.
segments.s214-g15: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd singular). بنزدیک ایران is a locative prepositional phrase 'near Iran' (بنزدیک = 'near, close to'); in epic/poetic Persian the noun سپاه ('the host/army') follows, so the canonical order is inverted: 'near Iran came the host' → 'the host came near Iran'.
segments.s214-g16: سری پر ز کینه is a nominal phrase 'a head full of hatred' (سری = 'head', پر + ز = 'full of', کینه = 'hatred'). دلی پرگناه parallels it as 'a heart full of sin' (دلی here denotes 'heart/heart‑felt' and پرگناه = 'full of sin / sinful'). These two noun‑phrases describe the state/attitude of the host; the copula is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s214-g33: This is a descriptive nominal clause: 'علف تنگ' (lit. ‘grass tight’) describes the state of the field, followed by the copula 'بود' in past tense. 'اندران' is a locative adverb meaning ‘in/therein’, and 'رزمگاه' names the place — word order is adjective-noun + verb + locative (typical of older Persian narrative).
segments.s214-g34: Verb phrase in the past tense: 'کشیدم' is first-person past ‘I drew/led’. 'ازان' (from that) gives the source/place; the preposition 'بر' here means ‘toward/against’, and 'هماون' (that same) points to the exact spot — finally 'سپاه' is the object ‘army/host’. The sequence shows movement from that location toward the same place (i.e. he led his host there).
segments.s214-g35: Adverb + verbal clause: 'کنون' = 'now'. 'آگهی شد' is a common classical passive/impersonal construction meaning ‘news came / it was announced’. 'بشاه جهان' is dative/address: 'به شاهِ جهان' = 'to the king of the world'. The phrase literally: 'Now information happened to the king of the world' → 'Now the king was informed.'
segments.s214-g36: This short clause announces the occasion. 'بیاید' is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used in classical narrative sense to mark the coming/arrival of an appointed time ('the time comes' / 'the time had come'). The doubled 'زمان ... زمان' is emphatic (the word 'زمان' repeated for force), 'تا' here functions idiomatically (emphatic/associative, roughly 'even/the very'), and 'ناگهان' is the adverb 'suddenly'.
segments.s214-g37: Noun phrase + verb + predicate: 'بزرگان لشکر' = 'the great ones (chiefs) of the army'. 'شدند' is past plural of 'شدن' meaning ‘became’ or here idiomatically ‘met / assembled’. 'انجمن' is the predicate nominative ‘an assembly/gathering’. Word order places the subject before the verb, then the predicate noun.
segments.s214-g38: 'چو' (short form of 'چنان‌که') = 'like/as'. The line lists comparisons: the first name 'دستان' is used as a proper name here (not the common noun senses listed), 'و' = 'and', the second 'چون' again 'like', 'رستم' is the personal name Rostam, and 'پیلتن' is a place-name epithet attached to Rostam. The phrase means they gathered like those named heroes.
segments.s214-g39: 'چو' = 'when'. 'جنبیدن' is the infinitival/masdar form 'to stir/move' used after 'چو' to set the temporal clause. 'شاه' is the subject of that action ('the king'), and 'کردم' is the speaker's verb 'I did/made' — here meaning 'I acted' or 'I took action' in response. 'درست' modifies 'کردم' as 'properly/straight/rightly'.
segments.s214-g40: This clause states the speaker's refusal to remain: 'نمانم' is negative of 'ماندن' in a fused form often used in narrative to mean 'I did not remain / I would not remain'. 'بتوران' is a prepositional phrase 'in/among Turan' (to Turan). 'بر و بوم' is a fixed collocation meaning 'land/soil (homeland)'; 'بر' = 'on/upon', 'بوم' = 'soil/homeland'. The final 'رست' is a past verbal/adjectival form meaning 'was saved/escaped/delivered'.

sentences.s214-s21: Now behold how the deeds of men come to pass; this is no time for deceit but a day of ambush.
sentences.s214-s22: When the elders heard, they sent out troops from every side and seized the mountain road.
sentences.s214-s23: A troop came from Turan toward that side, and the army gathered in assembly around the mountain.
sentences.s214-s24: When the grassy path became narrow for them, the spahbed turned toward finding a means for battle.
sentences.s214-s1: A messenger reached near Piran; Piran boiled with anger when he heard Homan speak.
sentences.s214-s2: Night came, dark at the hour of sleep; he drove the army in good order toward the water.
sentences.s214-s3: When the sun came from that pitch‑black veil, grief was rent and came forth.
sentences.s214-s4: The spahbed reached that very mountain; from the army's dust the mountain became unseen.
sentences.s214-s13: If you flee and then the army enters the foothills, you will surely fall into the snare.
sentences.s214-s14: Thus the proud Tūs replied: 'I have a misgiving about your lie.'
sentences.s214-s15: You have spread your hatred through the world, on account of Siavash, among the guests.
sentences.s214-s16: How long will you keep uttering lies in this way? May your falsehood not rob us of our splendor.
sentences.s214-s41: Tūs heard this speech from Goudarz; his head became full of old sorrow and hatred.
sentences.s214-s42: He entrusted one wing of the army to Bijan; the other side he (assigned) to Shidush and Kharad.
sentences.s214-s43: He spread (or caused to be spread) the auspicious standard, and he recalled many counsels and admonitions.
sentences.s214-s37: We must pick valiant horsemen to descend from the heights and attack toward the plain.
sentences.s214-s38: Let us make a fierce battle like a single night-raid, so that when fortune is our ally...
sentences.s214-s39: If we manage to kill one chieftain, or if we strip the proud of their crowns,
sentences.s214-s40: Such is the outcome of the battlefield: one gains the dust, another the crown — at times.
sentences.s214-s25: Homan said this to the elders: "We must be sent to the mountain."
sentences.s214-s26: Let us make one battle so that the Kayanids will not, from now on, close the quarrel between them.
sentences.s214-s27: The elders said to him: "It should be upon us — no one has learned warfare by the wind."
sentences.s214-s28: From the battle the infantry turned aside; the meeting became dark and hostile.
sentences.s214-s9: He shouted, "O famed Tus, lord of the elephants and of Gopāl and of the drums."
sentences.s214-s10: Now the fishes came in fives, so that you may ever seek battle for rice.
sentences.s214-s11: Of the Gudarzis, where are those who are senior? At that battlefield of yours, all are headless.
sentences.s214-s12: You, like a fury, went into the ranks at the waist; your heart full of justice, your head full of hatred.
sentences.s214-s33: Goudarz the old man said this to Tous: now war has become inevitable for us.
sentences.s214-s34: If there were food for three days, there is no more than that; from one side there is no open road ahead.
sentences.s214-s35: No food, no goods, no loads or bundles — if there are only so few, the host will remain hungry.
sentences.s214-s36: Now when the sun's face turns pale yellow, that lapis-colored tent appears.
sentences.s214-s29: When the grassy way grew narrow for the host, no one cast his gaze toward the rocky mountain.
sentences.s214-s30: If the whole host comes to our aid, from now on they will no longer seek battle with us.
sentences.s214-s31: Now is the time to pardon them; it is not the time for battle and for arraying (troops).
sentences.s214-s32: This message reached Goudarz and Tous, and the leaders stood aghast at the murmuring.
sentences.s214-s5: Behuman said thus: 'From the battlefield, do not stir — do not move, army, from here.'
sentences.s214-s6: Let me go so that I may see what the Kayanian star has in store for the commander of the Iranians.
sentences.s214-s7: To that mountain — which had given him the good news — what hope has he now of being here?
sentences.s214-s8: The host came near to Iran, its head full of hatred and its heart full of sin.
sentences.s214-s17: The grass was sparse on that battlefield; from there I drew my army to that very spot.
sentences.s214-s18: Now news came to the king of the world — the time had come, suddenly.
sentences.s214-s19: The chiefs of the host gathered into an assembly, like Dastān and like Rostam of Piltan.
sentences.s214-s20: When the king began to move, I acted rightly; I did not remain in Turan — I left its soil and was delivered.
